summary: Gruber argues that the DOJ's push to force Google to divest Chrome is fundamentally misguided from both business and technical perspectives. From a business standpoint, Chrome's value is entirely derived from being Google's integrated browser that eliminates traffic acquisition fees — it has no standalone commercial value. From a technical standpoint, Chrome is almost entirely open source via Chromium, meaning there's nothing proprietary to actually buy. He further argues that even if Google is banned from default search agreements, browser makers would likely keep Google as default anyway since the per-search payments are lucrative and users overwhelmingly choose Google. The piece concludes that Chrome is inseparable from Google itself — it's just a software layer between Google and its users, not a sellable asset.
key_points: Chrome saves Google $20-30 billion annually in traffic acquisition fees it would otherwise pay to a third-party browser owner, but this value evaporates the moment Chrome is separated from Google
The Chromium open source project means anyone can already build a Google-free Chrome — Microsoft, Brave, Vivaldi, and Opera already have, and none are very popular

Even if Google is banned from default search agreements, browser makers would rationally keep Google as default or present choice screens where users overwhelmingly pick Google anyway, as EU data shows
There is no plausible buyer who could both afford Chrome at a meaningful price and pass regulatory scrutiny
The DOJ's approach reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of how Chrome's value works — it's not a standalone product but an integrated layer of Google's ecosystem
money_quotes: 3 billion users = $15–$20 billion is not real math. It's just bullshit.
Chrome is extremely profitable for Google not because it makes any money on its own, but because every Google search that starts in Chrome is a search Google doesn't have to pay a TAC fee for.
They might as well try to force Google to sell the two o's from its name.
We know the value of a Google-free version of Chrome. Nothing. Zero.
The DOJ can't force Google to sell Chrome's user base because they're not Chrome users, per se, they're Google users.

Chrome does hold incredible value, but that value is inherent to Google and to Google/Chrome's users. It's not a standalone product with any commercial value whatsoever. It's just a software layer between Google and its users.

For chrissake Microsoft still owns and controls Windows and has made Edge — which I repeat is just a fork of Chromium — Windows's default browser and Edge has just 14% desktop market share and Chrome has 66%.
The more I think about it, the more it looks to me like a complete fantasy that Google even could sell Chrome.
key_insight: Chrome has no standalone commercial value because its worth is entirely derived from eliminating Google's traffic acquisition costs, and its codebase is already freely available as open source — making a forced divestiture both economically nonsensical and technically meaningless.
